channel.title: Discord Read Later
channel.description: URLs shared in Discord, ready for your RSS reader.

item.title: (中文配音)Anthropic 的Claude Code 創造者Boris Cherny 在紅杉AI Ascent 2026 上第一次講了他怎麼用loop - YouTube
谷歌资深工程师断言：代码审查已死！当AI产出翻4倍，程序员如何保住饭碗？ - YouTube
How This 5x Founder Runs His Startup Solo With AI Agents (OpenClaw, Codex, Devin) | Ryan Carson - YouTube
https://x.com/MaxBrodeurUrbas/status/2059736090937081980
item.description:
Post by @MaxBrodeurUrbas

Author: Max Brodeur-Urbas @MaxBrodeurUrbas Posted: 2026-05-27T20:38:46.000Z URL: https://x.com/MaxBrodeurUrbas/status/2059736090937081980 Likes: 229 | Retweets: 19

Post

We use agents for everything at Gumloop. This is not a brag. We're seeing efficiency boosts but it's also causing a whole new set of problems. We're experience "agentic growing pains" that a more standard company might only see in 6 months to a year from now. The most concerning issue is how easy it’s becoming to let agents get in between a person and their craft. The thing that made them exceptional before AI.

If you’re not making a conscious effort to stay human and close to your craft, you will slip. Using AI for everything is the path of least resistance once you have it connected to everything. We’ve been jokingly calling it the “slippery slop(e)”.

I put out a short memo in Slack a few weeks ago about it and thought I should convert it to a longer post.

Hey everyone

I wanted to flag something that Rahul and I were chatting about so that we can bake it into some sort of company policy or mantra.

Resist the slop, we win by staying human.

We’ve seen this issue come up a few times in certain customer interactions, in the way we’ve seen agents being used to communicate internally and in how some tasks are getting done in general.

  1. If something takes less time for you to create than for the recipient to consume, it’s probably slop

Creating content is unbelievably easy now. Brevity and clarity is all that matters. That is where the craft is.

I do not care if AI can make a 40 page slide deck for a presentation if the content could have been a single slack message. Time is so precious. Do not waste other people's time asking them to consume something you put minimal effort into.

Marrying AI content generation with high effort creativity is the magic. AI should shorten your journey to exceptional output instead of teleporting you to mediocrity.

  1. If our support doesn't feel deeply human, we're failing.

Everyone on the team provides support in one way or another so this is relevant to everyone. If we’re engaging in a support thread, the customer is already confused or annoyed. The single worst thing we can do is make them feel like they’re talking to a bot.

There was this old programming interview question I loved where you’d ask “write an algorithm to determine who won a tennis match when given all individual game scores”. Everyone would try to make an algo that counts points and attributes games to each player when the real answer is simple. If you just look at the last point scored, that’s who won.

You can read a support thread the same way. I like to scan customer channels, open threads and look at the last message. If it’s “thank you so much it worked great” I close it happy. If there’s no reply or a hanging “let me know if that works for you” from our side, I get the strong feeling the customer is not happy. Just because they went away does not mean they're satisfied.

Not replying to a thread is a very intentional act from the customer if they went so far out of their way to start that support thread. Using AI to reply will always be the easiest way to send the ball over the net but it does not make them want to play. They will realize they’re playing with a machine and will walk away. We’ll lose customers this way and entire accounts can die as a result.

  1. Internally, people want to talk to you, not your agent.

If you’re using an agent to interact with people on the team, you’re most likely wasting their time.

We specifically hired everyone on the team because we value working directly with you as people. Having your agent reply to coworkers messages is often times worse than not replying at all.

Pairing AI with the fact that everyone on this team is fucking cracked is how we win.

Top Comments

1. @taarichandaria

Author: Taari Chandaria Posted: 2026-05-28T08:33:45.000Z URL: https://x.com/taarichandaria/status/2059916022149234927

Refreshing take amidst all the tokenmaxxing discourse

Likes: 4

2. @JamesonZaballos

Author: Jameson Zaballos Posted: 2026-05-28T12:55:30.000Z URL: https://x.com/JamesonZaballos/status/2059981894586892676

“If something takes less time for you to create than for the recipient to consume, it’s probably slop”

Banger

Likes: 3

3. @KanyesThaker

Author: kanyes Posted: 2026-05-28T02:22:37.000Z URL: https://x.com/KanyesThaker/status/2059822623174050112

the product itself needs to be designed around this constraint though (long-term). can't rely on the user to police themselves. it's a hard problem, but i think very doable to design agents that are empowering without agency-draining

Likes: 2

4. @BradleyYoungjr

Author: Brad Posted: 2026-05-28T04:29:18.000Z URL: https://x.com/BradleyYoungjr/status/2059854503973724649

Man this was really refreshing to read. When consumption takes longer than creation 🔥

Likes: 1

5. @emtalas

Author: Emily Talas Posted: 2026-05-28T11:55:38.000Z URL: https://x.com/emtalas/status/2059966827862577292

Beautifully written!

Likes: 1

Shared: https://x.com/MaxBrodeurUrbas/status/2059736090937081980

item.title: Modern Engineering Values | Christoph Nakazawa
item.description:
‹ home
Table of Contents
› How I use LLMs Now
› Modern Engineering Values
› Strong Ownership
› Taste, Taste, Taste
› Strict Guardrails & Fast Feedback Loops
› Context in the Repo
› Own your Stack
› Option Value
› On Management
› How much faster?

Modern Engineering Values

Published on June 3, 2026
14 minutes reading time, 2700 words

At the end of last year I shared my LLM workflow in You are absolutely right!?. I knew it would be outdated quickly, but I didn’t realize it would be outdated that quickly.

I actually cannot believe that I rarely write code by hand anymore. Or rather, I cannot believe that I used to write code by hand! Programming has fundamentally changed and I’ve been wondering which engineering values still matter.

Before I get started, and to avoid criticism that I haven’t shipped anything, here are my contributions over the past several months:

  • Vite+: Built features in Rust for the launch (I don’t even know Rust), 90% AI-written
  • fate 1.0: Added live views, drizzle & GraphQL support, a Vite plugin, and garbage collection, 100% AI-written
  • Codiff: A fast, minimal, and beautiful diff review app, 100% AI-written
  • Athena Crisis: New features and 70+ bugfixes, 100% AI-written
  • Void: Metaframework and Cloud platform (Not yet shipped), 100% AI-written

Overall, I’ve found that coding agents now write code that is as good as or better than what I would write, and they do it in minutes instead of weeks. Since coding is no longer the bottleneck, I get a lot of work done that otherwise wouldn’t have happened at all1I even get to do silly things, like “VoidZero plays Pokémon”, a custom ROM hack that worked over websockets, in about five prompts, for our internal All Hands.

Most of my work is public and you can validate my work directly. I’m not bragging, I’m simply astonished. There has never been a developer experience improvement of this magnitude that allowed me to ship higher quality software so much faster.

Take Athena Crisis for example: It’s a pre-AI codebase that I wrote by hand. I haven’t had time to work on it lately so I had Codex run in a loop to identify bugs and fix them. It fixed 70 bugs, implemented new features, and wrote tests – and I was able to do all that while I was waiting on agents in other projects! As a result, Athena Crisis is now faster, more stable, and has more features than ever before. On top of all that it streamlined CI steps, improved the Steam app upload pipeline, wrote tests for some complex multi-user interactions, and I had it make a custom app to compare 200 pixel art icons with new variants to give the game a new look. None of this would have happened without coding agents.

How I use LLMs Now

Last year I didn’t run models locally, mostly isolated context to a specific module or function, and generated multiple versions for each solution.

Now, I’m using the Codex CLI, with GPT 5.5 high.2I prefer the CLI because it starts with a clean slate. There is no sidebar with old chats I don’t need, and I prefer one window per project instead of all projects in one window. It consistently one-shots correct solutions with very little prompting or structure, as long as the guardrails in the codebase are strong. I found that lower reasoning results in poor quality, and xhigh is too slow and overcomplicates things. I tried various other models, but nothing is as fast, as correct, or as easy to work with as Codex.3I appreciate everyone working on local models, and have high hopes for all of them to converge eventually!

I am ineffective at running multiple agent sessions in the same project (with worktrees, copies, or other solutions). I still review all code, and juggling orthogonal code changes in the same project slows me down. Context switching allows me to scale myself better and I can effectively work on multiple projects simultaneously. I usually have one Ghostty window per project, with a Codex tab on the left and a terminal on the right. Now I can work on 3-6 projects at a time, and the bottleneck is usually discussing and reviewing code.

All this multitasking added a spatial dimension to my work. I used to love just working from anywhere using my laptop, but now I prefer having a large screen available and assigning parts of the screen to various projects. For example, fate always ends up in the top center of the screen, void on the left side, and Athena Crisis on the right. Somehow the spatial assignment helps me multitask more effectively.

In terms of prompts, I’m pretty lazy. I often start new sessions for new topics (although context management matters much less with Codex these days) and ask “I want to build X. Get context from Y and Z, make a proposal, and ask me any questions you might have”. I iterate on the proposal by talking to the agent before asking it to execute on the plan.

When fixing bugs, I point it at a repro or context and force it to first create a failing test. This significantly increases the likelihood of the model fixing the right problem in the right way. Otherwise models tend to fix the wrong thing, make the wrong fix, or write the wrong test.

Keeping models on a tight leash is still the best method to get good or great solutions out of them. They still go off the rails and make many mistakes, and sometimes it’s better to kill the session and start from scratch. For larger or more complex changes I run a few /review cycles focusing on different areas to make sure the code is high-quality, bug free, and matches the plan. Once I validate the functionality (visually, functional, or with tests, etc.) I review the code using Codiff4Install via brew install --cask nkzw-tech/tap/codiff!, usually by typing codiff -w which uses Codex to create a walkthrough of the uncommitted changes. I sometimes ship code that I don’t study closely – if the tests look good, the code structure looks fine, and is in a less critical area that can be changed easily. In general, agents don’t make the type of mistakes humans used to make, and at this point I put more scrutiny on hand-written code than on agent-written code.

The best next step is to push to main. Unfortunately, that’s not how most teams operate, so once you enter the PR or collaboration phase you kind of lose most of the velocity that LLMs give you. I believe that all technical organizations will have to drastically change and break down barriers to getting things done if they want to be successful going forward.

Modern Engineering Values

I’ve been trying to wrap my head around engineering values and principles that matter for 2026 and beyond that will help individuals and organizations move faster. Looking at my engineering workflow it feels like on one hand everything changed, and on the other, not that much.

I thought about how individual people operate and how it differs from big tech. A lot of infrastructure that small teams are building now is similar to the type of infrastructure that big tech has had for many years. It’s funny when people call out how much slop agents are producing. They must have never seen the slop engineers produce at big tech! Other people call out: How can you be effective when you don’t understand the code? Guess what, at big tech, most people don’t understand much of the code.

I don’t think programming or code will go away. Markdown files with specs written in English is a terrible programming language full of ambiguity. Dynamic code with agents seems like a much more sensible approach. Engineering is becoming less about producing code and more about directing systems that produce code. Things will stay closer to what they are than most people think.

So how do you move fast and what are the values that matter? Regardless of whether the engineers are human or AI, I think the values are very similar:

Strong Ownership

The best engineers exhibit strong ownership and domain expertise in the area they work in. Agents amplify ownership. Someone who deeply understands a product can now execute faster, while someone without context can generate much more noise. As a result, coordination becomes more expensive. I believe the most effective teams will be small, often two to three people, with clear ownership boundaries and isolated repositories.5This is painful to me as I was always a big proponent of monorepos and helped bring workspaces to JavaScript!

Strong ownership means understanding the architecture, product requirements, tradeoffs, and long-term direction of a system. It means reviewing the work produced by agents, making decisions confidently, and sometimes pushing directly to main.

Code reviews on small teams should focus on alignment, not on arguments about code. They should only be necessary when there are open questions that haven’t already been discussed.

Taste, Taste, Taste

I have a low tolerance for bullshit, and with agents, everyone can generate a lot of bullshit all day long. Please just stop. Good taste is not just about building great products, it’s about what makes it worth spending your time on something in the first place. I recommend engineering teams to spend more time on figuring out what is worth spending time on.

Strict Guardrails & Fast Feedback Loops

To keep velocity up, large organizations tend to put heavy constraints on what code should look like. Working with coding agents is basically the same as working in a large organization: You drop new engineers without context on the codebase all the time, just like agents! The more constraints like lint rules, automated testing, and other methods of fast verification you put on the code, the faster people can iterate on their changes, and the faster they can get work done.

Fast and scalable tooling matters even more with agents than before. Tighter feedback loops make it faster for agents to iterate and complete their work. Because the amount of code produced is accelerating, it’s important for tools to operate on changed files instead of the whole repo and you don’t want tools to become slower as the lines of code grow.

Strict guardrails and fast feedback loops are the difference between an agent completing their work in 1 minute or 60.

Context in the Repo

There was a recent argument (or joke?) about how tests are the context, or how design docs are the context, and how it might be worth keeping that context “secret” from other parties. The big realization is that in the past, context was scattered across many places:

  • Notion / Google Docs
  • People
  • Implicit context (Not written down anywhere or forgotten)
  • How the product actually behaves in production
  • Commit messages
  • Code

Since each agent session is like a new employee, likely without access to all these places to gather context, the best thing is to put all the relevant context together, locally, in the repo. This is a great place to inject taste, values, principles and your own thinking. Do not let agents put slop all over your codebase. The great thing about doing this is that it will make the codebase more accessible for agents and humans both!

Similarly, while we might look at individual lines of code less than we used to, there is even more value in making sure code is optimized for reading. Simpler and less code is easier to understand and fix.

Own your Stack

Historically, you might have built apps where 95% of the code was third-party dependencies. It made sense to depend on external libraries because writing and maintaining code was slow and expensive. However, it also locked you into a stack you didn’t control. The data fetching library or third-party UI framework might have dictated the downstream user experience.

Every dependency introduces code, architecture, constraints, and decisions that you are ultimately responsible for, regardless of who wrote them. They make it feel like you’re shipping a small application when in reality you’re shipping a large body of code that you didn’t write and may not fully understand. Agents change these cost factors and reduce complexity.

Over the past several years I built my own open source JavaScript stack around data libraries, i18n, UI libraries, tool configs, starter templates, slideshows and more. It allows me to spin up new projects with tight constraints for agents quickly, and more importantly, it gives me full control over the product and user experience. With agents, there is no longer a good reason to outsource core parts of your product to third-party dependencies if you can realistically own them yourself.

Option Value

I wrote about Option Value in Principles of Developer Experience:

[This principle] is about retaining or gaining option value, which means any change to a system should unlock more options for improvements and significant future changes. […] If we keep option value in mind when redesigning infrastructure, we can naturally adapt to new requirements in the future.

AI fundamentally changes how long it takes to make large-scale changes. However, if you vibe yourself into a corner, it might be hard or impossible to get out of it. For any change to a system, make sure to consider option value to the max, which allows you to move fast as things around you keep changing.

On Management

I have been reflecting on what modern engineering management looks like now. There were fundamental human limits to team sizes, scope, and management layers before. What we are seeing now, and what we’ll continue to see, is these limits being pushed. Some companies will get this wrong in one way or another in the short-term, but directionally I believe that engineering management is going to become more technical, not less. Of course, in most tech companies, the engineering managers are or used to be technical already. However, many of them, especially once they enter middle management, tend to lose touch with “the code”.

As execution becomes cheaper and individual contributors gain more leverage, managers can no longer just own outcomes or direction. They have to retain domain expertise in the area they work in, be able to make changes to their projects confidently, and provide technical leadership. I wrote about this years ago in Tech Lead Management, and I’m convinced this role is the right one for most managers in the agent era.

How much faster?

Ok, but how much faster can you move with agents? Looking at my GitHub contributions in the past 30 days, I pushed 770 commits and modified 15k lines of code on average each day (312k added, 154k removed, including days I didn’t code). Compared to the same time a year earlier, I shipped 328 commits, and modified about 5k lines of code each day (3k added, 2k removed). 2024 looks roughly similar to 2025. This means I made more than twice as many commits, and I shipped 3 times as much code as in the same period two years prior.6Of course, commits and lines of code are normally a bad metric, but I’m comparing my productivity to my past self, and I have roughly worked on projects in the same space.

My best days when writing code by hand were about 1200 lines a day. I had like one of those a week. Now I ship ten times more, at a higher quality. It’s more intense, faster, and addictive – but I genuinely feel like I move fast with stable infrastructure.

People are concerned about their jobs. Sometimes I feel like I’m just carrying context from one agent session into a GitHub Issue, or into Discord, and back to the agent. However, unless models receive innate motivation aligned with my goals and taste it is unlikely that I’ll be cut out of this process anytime soon.

In fact, I feel like the opposite is true. We are unlocking the ability to ship amazing things at lightning speed, and we need more people than ever to do it. I was bottlenecked on writing code, now I’m bottlenecked on exercising judgement. Let’s ship!

Thanks for reading.

Related Posts

You are absolutely right!?
ai
Frontend Engineer Archetypes
article
Building a JavaScript Bundler
guide

Shared: https://cpojer.net/posts/modern-engineering-values

item.title: https://x.com/patrick_oshag/status/2054532117410054252
item.description:
Post by @patrick_oshag

Author: Patrick OShaughnessy @patrick_oshag Posted: 2026-05-13T12:00:02.000Z URL: https://x.com/patrick_oshag/status/2054532117410054252 Likes: 6,810 | Retweets: 803

Post

Krishna Rao is the CFO of Anthropic, and this is his first podcast appearance.

He joined the company two years ago when run-rate revenue was about $250M. Today it is $30B. He has helped raise ~$75B and is responsible for the procurement and allocation of compute.

I feel lucky we get to hear what it is like to sit inside a company this consequential at a moment this pivotal.

We discuss:

  • The cone of uncertainty
  • How he allocates compute across Trainium, TPUs, and GPUs
  • What investors misunderstand about model companies
  • Why the returns to frontier intelligence keep rising
  • Platform vs application and where Anthropic builds its own products
  • How Anthropic uses Claude internally

I have asked my closing question about the kindest thing more than 500 times. Krishna's answer is one I have never heard before.

Enjoy!

Timestamps: 0:00 Intro 2:38 The Compute Canvas 6:51 The "Cone of Uncertainty" 11:58 Why the Returns to Frontier Intelligence Are So High 16:45 Recursive Self-Improvement 20:20 Scaling Laws 23:30 Sourcing $100 Billion in Compute 28:05 Platform vs. Application Strategy 32:52 Pricing Dynamics 38:48 How Anthropic’s Finance Team Uses Claude 43:24 Raising Capital & Overcoming Investor Skepticism 52:32 Public Perception, Risks, and Government Regulation 57:25 Mythos Release 1:12:33 What Could Derail the AI Revolution? 1:13:47 Biotech and Healthcare 1:15:31 The Kindest Thing

Top Comments

1. @InvestLikeBest

Author: Invest Like the Best Posted: 2026-05-13T17:29:39.000Z URL: https://x.com/InvestLikeBest/status/2054615068189286898

Apple: https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/krishna-rao-anthropics-cfo-on-managing-compute-scaling/id1154105909?i=1000767545842

Spotify: https://open.spotify.com/episode/5aqjRClzztuVmXEdGz281O?si=SZllNkGCSxu7dNSJ3GnoUA

Likes: 25

2. @thebeautyofsaas

Author: BOSS Posted: 2026-05-13T12:53:35.000Z URL: https://x.com/thebeautyofsaas/status/2054545591250649532

what a ridiculously interesting (and different) selection of guests. thank you

Likes: 18

3. @Mosumon8

Author: Mohammed Sumon Posted: 2026-05-13T15:37:06.000Z URL: https://x.com/Mosumon8/status/2054586743001592177

Great podcast. Separately -- as I listened to this without video, I genuinely could not tell if I was hearing Patrick or Krishna...

Likes: 16

4. @Haoda_Song

Author: Haoda (Steven) Song Posted: 2026-05-13T12:15:55.000Z URL: https://x.com/Haoda_Song/status/2054536113214161061

The compute allocation piece is where the real leverage sits. Most CFOs manage money, but Krishna's essentially managing the constraint that actually limits AI companies right now.

Likes: 11

5. @InvestLikeBest

Author: Invest Like the Best Posted: 2026-05-13T12:10:17.000Z URL: https://x.com/InvestLikeBest/status/2054534694134579427

Ful episode on Youtube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?si=Ccirfjv8M0RngWMw&v=wEEZPpx8qow&feature=youtu.be

Likes: 8

Shared: https://x.com/patrick_oshag/status/2054532117410054252

item.title: Interfaces › Design Engineering Magazine
item.description:
Interfaces › Design Engineering Magazine

Interfacesx-heightoklch(0.991 0 0)oklch(0.173 0 0)

The Design Engineering Magazinefont-libre-baskerville

by Jakub Krehel
32 px
Subscribe Now
Call To Action
Subscribe Now
Call To Action

What Makes an Interface Feel Great?

I think a lot about what makes an interface feel great.

When you go from using a good product to a great one, you can feel the difference. It's often hard to point to what makes it though.

It's usually not a single thing, but instead a lot of small things that add up.

wrap

Designing interfacesDesigning interfaces that feel natural and intuitive

Great design is invisible. It guides users without them ever noticing.

balance

Designing interfacesDesigning interfaces that feel natural and intuitive

Great design is invisible. It guides users without them ever noticing.

Width220px
padding: 8px
12px
12px
20px
12px

In the age of artificial intelligence, shipping fast is no longer an advantage. When anything can be replicated in minutes, just building something isn't enough.

Products that are going to stand out and last are those that are built extremely well, with intent and extraordinary care.

That's what Interfaces focuses on.

About Interfaces

I love writing and sharing what I learn. Articles on my website have been read more than one million times. It makes me very happy that people find them useful.

Interfaces is where I share everything I know and continue to learn about building great interfaces.

I cover a variety of topics from animation and typography to layout, color and everything else that is a part of building a great interface.

Using Gestures in MotionApril 2026
Understanding GradientsMarch 2026
What Are OKLCH Colors?February 2026
How I Use Shared Layout AnimationsJanuary 2026

How It Works

Once you subscribe, you get full access to the Interfaces platform. This includes all previously released issues with interactive demos and code snippets.

At least one new issue is released every month.

You also get access to a curated library of resources, tools I personally use, agent skills, and a private Discord community.

April 2026
May 2026
June 2026
July 2026

New issue every month

At least one new issue is released every month.

Border Radius14px

Interactive demos

Concepts are explained through interactive demos instead of text only.

Source code

Most examples and demos include the source code.

/interfaces
Coming Soon
/make-interfaces-feel-better
/oklch-skill

Agent skills

Skill covering many of the topics explored in the magazine.

Bookmarks and tools

A curated library of resources and tools I personally use.

Private Discord community

Discord community to discuss issues and meet like-minded people.

Nice to meet you, I'm Jakub

I'm a founding design engineer at Interfere. I write articles about a variety of design engineering topics on my website and I built oklch.fyi.

I care deeply about craft and quality and I like to make people feel something through my work. I'm obsessed with details and I often spend an unreasonable amount of time on them.

Previously, I worked at OpenSea and a few other companies. You might have seen some of my work on Twitter or my code on GitHub.

jakub.kr
BlogMy personal website.
oklch.fyiOKLCH color generator & converter.

Start Building Great Interfaces

Quality matters more than ever. If you care about building great interfaces, you like to sweat the details and want to learn more, I think you’ll enjoy the magazine.

Monthly

$7.99/ per month

  • New issue every month

  • Resource library

  • Interactive demos

  • Source code

  • Agent skills

  • Private Discord community

Subscribe
Save 18%

Yearly

$79.99/ per year

  • New issue every month

  • Resource library

  • Interactive demos

  • Source code

  • Agent skills

  • Private Discord community

Subscribe

Read three issues, free

Get a feel for Interfaces. These issues are free to read and give you a glimpse into what the magazine is about.

Details that make interfaces feel better
How I use shared layout animations
Understanding gradients

Newsletter

About

interfaces.dev

item.title: Notion | Where teams and agents work together
item.description:
Notion

A collaborative AI workspace, built on your company context. Build and orchestrate agents right alongside your team

Shared: https://spark-path-418.notion.site/AI-2026-37b31f65c48880c6b2b1fa851bf85b1b

item.title: 如何把「善用 AI」寫到履歷中?
item.description:
ExplainThis 軟體工程白話聊 · Facebook

如何把「善用 AI」寫到履歷中? 󠀠 因為近期使用 AI 這項能力逐漸成為軟體工程師的標配，許多公司在找新員工時，也會評估對 AI 工具使用的精熟程度。在 ExplainThis 經營的 E+ 社群當中，也有許多讀者陸續在履歷上面寫跟使用 AI 相關的列點。 󠀠 就寫列點來說，如果要讓看履歷的人感受到「這個候選人善用 AI」，核心關鍵是要寫出相關的關鍵字 (所以 ATS 能抓到)，以及要寫出成果影響力 (所以用人主管會想找來面試)。 󠀠 公司在評估工程師時，不只會問有沒有用過 AI 工具 (現在幾乎多數公司都預設工程師有在用)，而是會進一步評估「怎麼用? 多有效?」。換句話說，有沒有將 AI 運用到多個環節、用了後產生多大的影響力，這些都會是評估的方向。因此，不能只是寫一句「熟悉 AI 工具」而已，而是讓人看見你怎麼把 AI 變成工作流程的一部分，並且有實際的成果。 󠀠 如果要進一步區分初階與資深工程師使用 AI 的方式，那麼只停留在用 AI 寫程式、寫測試，這種最基本的列點，會比較難與初階工程師拉開區別。能否在用 AI 時對接到適當的外部工具、能否透過代理技能 (agent skills) 來提升整個團隊的品質與一致性、能否透過子代理來加速、能否在用 AI 的同時最小化 tokens 消耗成本，都會是評估的面向。 󠀠 即使這些平常都有做到，如果在呈現上沒有修飾，也可能會看起來沒區別度。以下我們會透過三個實際列點，來談可以如何寫更好。 󠀠 範例一「會用 AI 做 Code Review」 󠀠 首先，如果履歷上寫「使用 AI 協助 Code Review，提升程式碼品質」，這會讓人知道你懂得用 AI 來把關程式碼品質，但這幾乎是人人都能寫出的點。 󠀠 更理想的寫法會是 「設計複利工程工作流程，讓 AI 代理自動萃取每次互動的模式並融入技能庫，使 Code Review 重複性意見減少 XX%，審核來回次數從 XX 次降至 YY 次」。 󠀠 英文版本則是 Designed a compound engineering workflow that automatically extracts patterns from AI agent conversations into a reusable skill library, reducing repetitive cod

Shared: https://m.facebook.com/story.php?story_fbid=pfbid0YfVL9eGpiY4VdUEXzANNMGKQtyU7WZDyHACqruBxM8thZ3iEYkqpsXDRefGLgXKzl&id=100089328909701&mibextid=wwXIfr

Claude Code plugin for designing modular systems from functional requirements and reviewing existing codebases for modularity problems with actionable improvement recommendations. Based on the Bala...

item.title: GitHub - ParthJadhav/app-store-screenshots: end to end app store screenshot creation using AI

GitHub

end to end app store screenshot creation using AI. Contribute to ParthJadhav/app-store-screenshots development by creating an account on GitHub.

Shared: https://github.com/ParthJadhav/app-store-screenshots

item.title: 當 TDD 不再是品質保證，而是 AI 的操控介面
item.description:
劉詩雁 · Facebook

當 TDD 不再是品質保證，而是 AI 的操控介面 Simon Willison 上週寫了一篇關於 Red/Green TDD 用在 coding agent 上的短文，核心主張很直覺：先寫測試、確認失敗、再讓 AI 寫到通過，這樣 coding agent 就不會亂寫一通。文章寫得清楚，觀察也對。但我覺得它卡在一個常見的盲點：它把 TDD 當作一種「品質保證手段」在講，卻沒追問一個更根本的問題，當你用測試來驅動 AI 寫程式，你其實正在重新定義「寫程式」這件事本身的價值結構。 表面上看，這是一篇關於 prompt engineering 的技巧文。但從產業經濟的角度看，這更像是一個訊號：軟體開發的價值正在從「生成程式碼」遷移到「定義驗收條件」。 先拆解 TDD 在 AI 語境下到底改變了什麼。傳統的 TDD 是開發者自律的工具，你先想清楚行為規格，再動手實作，避免寫出不必要的東西。但當執行實作的不是人而是 coding agent，TDD 的角色就從「自律工具」變成了「控制介面」。測試不再只是驗證程式對不對，它變成了你跟 AI 溝通「我到底要什麼」的唯一精確語言。 這件事的意義比看起來大得多。過去十年，軟體工程師的核心產出是程式碼。程式碼是資產，寫得好的程式碼有維護性、可擴展性，這些是工程師薪資的錨點。但如果 coding agent 能在幾秒內生成程式碼，而且只要測試寫得夠好就能保證品質，那「寫程式碼」這個動作的邊際價值會被壓到趨近於零。剩下來有價值的，是寫測試的能力——更精確地說，是把模糊的商業需求翻譯成可執行驗收條件的能力。 用一個粗暴的算法來感受這件事： - 傳統模式下，一個功能的開發時間分配：需求釐清 20%、寫測試 15%、寫實作 50%、debug 15% - AI 輔助模式下（沒有 TDD）：需求釐清 20%、寫實作 0%（AI 生成）、review AI 產出 + debug 60% - AI + Red/Green TDD 模式：需求釐清 30%、寫測試 40%、AI 實作 5%、驗證通過 25% 注意那個結構性的翻轉：實作時間從佔一半壓縮到近乎消失，但「定義什麼叫做對」的時間從 35% 膨脹到 70%。開發者的工作內容沒有消失，但重心徹底搬家了。 這帶出真正值得追問的幾件事： 如果「寫測試」變成核心技能，軟體人才的估值邏

Shared: https://www.facebook.com/modeerf/posts/pfbid0SyYzCmXntBJzS8kzqQXTQokVM9aC3QjMrorrgyJmZ6dQzEjuge3CKLCqdhz191Pgl?locale=zh_TW

item.title: 三年前讓我在休耕期破戒推薦的一本書《數值化之鬼》的作者安藤廣大要來台灣開課了。
item.description:
游舒帆 · Facebook

三年前讓我在休耕期破戒推薦的一本書《數值化之鬼》的作者安藤廣大要來台灣開課了。 我本身是個數據派，當初對於書中所提到的「數字能夠斷開情緒」、「數字能讓我們維持客觀」、「數字能讓彼此取得共識」等觀念我特別有感。 還記得我在演講與課程中經常問大家：「為什麼營收與客戶滿意度無法比較？」 最常得到的答案是：「兩個本來就是不同的東西。」 不過有時也會有人回問：「難道只有營收重要，滿意度不重要嗎？」 而這兩個回覆的背後，其實反應出來的議題就是「缺乏共識」。 營收與滿意度為什麼無法比較？從數學與邏輯的角度來回答，我的答案是：「單位不同。」 身高與體重單位不同，所以無法比較，若要讓兩者有關連性，但兩者能否有關係？可以的，你可以用 BMI 這個數值來讓兩者產生關係。當你要降低 BMI 數值時，你可以選擇增加身高，或者降低體重。 藉由引入另一個數值來讓兩個不同單位的數值產生關連，你就可以「選擇」要改變哪個數值。可以選擇，意味著可以「決策」。 我也經常會問一個問題：「一個人 170 公分是高還是矮？」 聽眾腦袋中冒出的念頭可能是「要看男生還女生」、「可能跟年紀有關」、「看台灣人還是歐美人士」。聽到這些回覆我很高興，因為大家都清楚一件事 - 一個數值單獨存在，沒有比較是沒有意義的。 對台灣/成年/男性來說，170 公分略低平均值，稱不上高，但對台灣/成年/女性來說，170 公分則高於平均將近 10 公分。 跟誰比？這是所謂的「比較基準(Baseline)」的議題。 不同的比較基準，會得出不一樣的答案。看目標達成率，是跟目標相比，看成長率，是跟上一期相比，看年複合成長則是跟去年比，看 YoY 則是與去年同期比。不同的比較基準，呈現的管理議題是不同的。 還記得有一年有位行銷團隊的成員問我：「我們 YoY 成長了兩倍多，為什麼你不在意，你只看目標達成率？」 我告訴他：「因為去年我們連行銷團隊都沒有，今年我們不只組建了四人團隊，還投入了數百萬的行銷預算，YoY 成長是正常的，但我更關注總體名單數，平均 CPL。因為那才是我們今年努力要看到的成果。」 在不同的管理情境下，你看待數字的角度會有所不同。 那營收與滿意度到底能不能比較呢？可以的，但要換算成相同的單位。我的建議是統一換算成金額。 舉例來說，業務提出一個能帶動 5,000 萬收入的一次性專案，而客服則提出一

Shared: https://www.facebook.com/gipi.net/posts/pfbid02PQNyZ5uALVpgEJshbVMDYyp2seXWQZpQKF6n5TrAimqUDMTnZEJbwxwgZQjL71MLl?locale=zh_TW

item.title: 前 OpenAI 及 Tesla 核心成員 Andrej Karpathy 承認自己從去年12月開始就沒自己寫過code，近期接受 No Priors Podcast 訪談，分享他對 agent 時代的第一手觀察。從親身經歷的「AI 狂熱」狀態，到對自動化研究、開源生態與教育未來的深度思考，這場對話涵蓋了當前 AI 發展最前沿的諸多議題。
item.description:
IEObserve 國際經濟觀察 · Facebook

前 OpenAI 及 Tesla 核心成員 Andrej Karpathy 承認自己從去年12月開始就沒自己寫過code，近期接受 No Priors Podcast 訪談，分享他對 agent 時代的第一手觀察。從親身經歷的「AI 狂熱」狀態，到對自動化研究、開源生態與教育未來的深度思考，這場對話涵蓋了當前 AI 發展最前沿的諸多議題。 --- #從親手寫程式到「指揮 agent」 Karpathy 坦言，自去年 12 月起，他的工作方式發生了根本性的轉變。「我大概從去年 12 月開始就沒有親手打過一行程式碼了，這是一個極其巨大的改變。」他說，在那之前，他的工作模式大約是八成自己寫、兩成交給 agent，但如今這個比例已經完全翻轉，甚至遠不止於此。 這個改變讓他陷入一種他自稱的「AI 狂熱」（AI psychosis）狀態。「因為個人所能達到的成就出現了巨大的解鎖，你原本受限於自己的打字速度，但現在透過這些 agent，這個瓶頸已經消失了。」他描述自己每天都在思索：什麼是可能的？還有哪些邊界尚未被探索？「因為這一切在本質上仍是未知領域。」 他提到工程師 Peter Steinberg 的工作方式令他印象深刻。Peter 在螢幕前同時開著多個 Codex agent，每個 agent 負責一個獨立的 repo，大約花 20 分鐘完成一個任務，他在這些 agent 之間穿梭，分配工作、審核成果。 「你可以以更宏觀的層次行動，不再是寫這一行、寫這個函式，而是：這是一個新功能，交給 agent 1；這是另一個不會干擾的新功能，交給 agent 2，然後盡力審查它們的產出。」 #Token吞吐量：新時代的「GPU 使用率」 Karpathy 把管理 agent 的感受比擬為他讀博士班時看 GPU 使用率的焦慮感。「我如果看到訂閱額度還有剩，就代表我沒有把 token 吞吐量最大化，這讓我感到不安。」他說，如果一個 agent 的額度用完了，就立刻切換到另一個服務繼續跑，目標是讓計算資源持續被充分利用。 這種思維轉變意味著在這個新系統中，人本身才是效率的瓶頸。「這反而讓人很有動力，因為你還有成長空間，每次找到新的使用方式就會有新的解鎖，所以才會讓人如此上癮。」 #Agent的人格：Claude vs. Codex Karpathy 特別強調 agent

Shared: https://www.facebook.com/intleconobserve/posts/pfbid0KGfULDWvA53GRacB6jVSkvCfRs7rggJHQQDocNrYNyPiHYVaE5AjXAkvYdzwnRJxl?locale=zh_TW

item.title: Claude Code 51 萬行洩漏：我們真的看見了一個 Agent OS 長什麼樣子 ，其實要說技術還好，雖然也是神仙打架級別，但設計哲學更是精妙。
item.description:
Si Javitong · Facebook

Claude Code 51 萬行洩漏：我們真的看見了一個 Agent OS 長什麼樣子 ，其實要說技術還好，雖然也是神仙打架級別，但設計哲學更是精妙。 最近大家應該都有看到「Claude Code 原始碼意外裸奔」的新聞。 有趣的是，這次洩漏不只是一則八卦也不是愚人節玩笑🤣，更等於免費送了一份「生產等級 AI Agent 系統的完整工程實作範本」——對正在做 Agent 開發的人來說，這可能比任何一篇技術博文都更有參考價值，畢竟平常哪有機會這樣近距離觀摩別人的產品內臟。 下面用比較白話的方式，整理幾個最值得關注的重點。 ◆ 這次洩漏到底是怎麼回事？ 不是駭客入侵，而是 npm 發布包意外夾帶了一個超大的 source map 檔案，裡面的 sourcesContent 直接包含完整 TypeScript 原始碼，等於把設計圖一起打包出門。 3 月 31 日早上，安全研究員 Chaofan Shou 在 X 上公開了這個發現，貼文在幾小時內累積千萬等級觀看。研究者從中重建出超過 51 萬行生產級程式碼，約 1900 個檔案，包含完整的 Agent runtime、工具系統、多 Agent 調度、安全與權限架構。 官方緊急下架重發，但程式碼已被多方備份，鏡像 repo 在短時間內累積數萬顆星與 fork。 換句話說，這不只是「有趣的 bug」，更像是： Anthropic 不小心把他們對「AI Agent 應該長什麼樣子」的設計哲學，完整攤在桌上給大家看，還貼心附上檔案結構，方便全世界做讀書會。 ＃失誤者還在X上道歉說他被辭退了 （希望被辭退不是真的 ◆ Claude Code 不是一個 CLI 工具，而是一個 Agent Operating System 很多人第一眼以為 Claude Code 就是「把 Claude 接到 terminal 的 coding 助手」，結果打開目錄會發現整個世界觀不一樣。 整個系統大致可以拆成五層：入口層、Runtime 層、Engine 層、工具與能力層、基礎設施層，同一套 Agent runtime 同時服務 CLI、MCP 協議、SDK、IDE 插件。 近 1,900 個檔案，核心模組包含 QueryEngine.ts（約 4.6 萬行）、Tool.ts（約 2.9 萬行）

Shared: https://www.facebook.com/javitong/posts/pfbid0C9xc9a1hgsPYUHcCszLL6sidjbodDuyNbrH9eECiZUpDACtHNzj5u82ZxT8849EHl?locale=zh_TW

item.title: 工程師的日常開發，不管專案大小，流程大概都長這樣：
item.description:
網頁開發雜記 · Facebook

工程師的日常開發，不管專案大小，流程大概都長這樣： 釐清需求 → 拆任務 → 實作 → 驗證 → code review → 部署 問題是，這個流程裡的每一步「該做到什麼程度」，依據每個人的習慣差異非常之大。新人或 AI agent 拿到任務，最常做的事就是跳過前面幾步直接開始寫 code。開發流程中的各階段都有其作用，如果有一個流程能讓各階段都能完好的進行，那對於產出品質絕對會有很大的幫助。 https://github.com/addyosmani/agent-skills Addy Osmani 最近分享的 agent-skills 這個 repo 就有這感覺，把開發流程拆成六個階段、19 個 skill： 1. Define：透過發散和收斂思考把模糊想法變成具體提案（idea-refine），再寫成結構化的 spec，用 gated workflow（Specify → Plan → Tasks → Implement）確保每個階段都要人確認才能往下走（spec-driven-development） 2. Plan：把 spec 拆成可獨立驗證的小任務，每個任務有明確的完成條件和依賴順序，而且要小到 agent 可以可靠地一次完成（planning-and-task-breakdown） 3. Build：最特別的是一次只做一小塊完整功能，支援 feature flag 和 rollback 的設計的incremental-implementation skill，其他包含 context-engineering、TDD、frontend-ui-engineering、api-and-interface-design 4. Verify：有 browser-testing-with-devtools 可以透過 Chrome DevTools MCP 讓 agent 直接看到瀏覽器裡的 DOM、console log、網路請求和效能數據。以及系統化的五步驟 debug 流程：重現 → 定位 → 簡化 → 修復 → 加防護（debugging-and-error-recovery） 5. Review：五個維度的 code review——正確性、可讀性、架構、安全、效能，還可以用不同 model 交叉 review（code-review-and-

Shared: https://www.facebook.com/thingsaboutwebdev/posts/pfbid02Xr5U3r8b9L9Ev3GUGXEDgLA7vJB1NWtF8GDC7dShLW9b3wUmoaQtEu9ziq1gCrbil?locale=zh_TW

item.title: 比Ollama還快上4.2倍？！榨乾 Mac 的新玩具來啦！
item.description:
LargitData - 大數軟體 · Facebook

比Ollama還快上4.2倍？！榨乾 Mac 的新玩具來啦！ Rapid-MLX以MLX框架為底層，善用 Metal 運算核心與統一記憶體零拷貝存取，18 個模型中有 16 個拿下推論冠軍。 最亮眼的是針對Qwen3.5設計快取加速技巧。Qwen3.5採混合架構，其中75%是RNN結構，傳統KV Cache每次都得重算；Rapid-MLX 在系統提示這個固定不變的位置，把RNN內部狀態整個拍照存下來，下次請求只要0.1毫秒就能還原快照，把Qwen3-Coder-Next的首字延遲從0.68 秒壓到0.14秒，快了4.8倍。 另一個亮點是工具呼叫可靠性。內建 17 種解析器與自動恢復機制，當4-bit量化模型在多輪呼叫後格式崩壞，引擎會自動把純文字還原成結構化 tool_calls，確保 100% 成功率。 各種機型都能跑：16GB MacBook Air跑Qwen3.5-4B 4bit達158 tok/s；64GB Mac Mini能流暢執行 Qwen3.5-35B-A3B 8bit維持80 tok/s；128GB 以上的Mac Studio則能挑戰Qwen3.5-122B。

Shared: https://www.facebook.com/largitdata/posts/pfbid0zXNH6U7fiVpXir4Dg3wHPatYxasFyiPD6GEmvjDe5tsrrEJBicCfNRYera3Wjf9yl?locale=zh_TW
